tasks.title: Implement CJK system font fallback for text rendering
tasks.description: Add platform-specific CJK font fallbacks so Korean, Chinese, and Japanese characters render correctly without boxes, preferring system fonts over bundling large CJK fonts.
tasks.details:
Scope:
- Implement Issue #7 core behavior using **system font fallback** as the primary approach, while remaining compatible with potential future decision to bundle fonts.

Implementation details:
- Work in `src/fonts.rs` and any font initialization code in the rendering stack.
- Before coding, check status of PR #8 (Noto Sans KR bundling) and align with its outcome (but for now, design assuming system fallback is preferred and bundling can be added later without breaking changes).

Font selection strategy:
- Define a cross-platform list of preferred CJK system fonts:
  - Windows: "Malgun Gothic", "Meiryo", "Microsoft YaHei".
  - macOS: "Apple SD Gothic Neo", "PingFang SC", "Hiragino Sans".
  - Linux: "Noto Sans CJK", "Noto Sans CJK KR", "Noto Sans CJK SC", plus generic "sans-serif".
- Keep existing Inter/JetBrains Mono for Latin text; augment font stack with CJK-capable fonts.

Rust-side design:
- If using a font-loading abstraction (e.g., wgpu/egui-like stack), create a helper in `fonts.rs`:
  ```rust
  pub fn register_fonts(ctx: &mut FontContext) {
      // existing Latin fonts
      ctx.add_font_from_bytes("Inter", include_bytes!("../assets/fonts/Inter-Regular.ttf"));
      ctx.add_font_from_bytes("JetBrains Mono", include_bytes!("../assets/fonts/JetBrainsMono-Regular.ttf"));

      // platform-specific CJK fallbacks
      let os = std::env::consts::OS;
      let cjk_candidates: &[&str] = match os {
          "windows" => &[
              "Malgun Gothic",
              "Meiryo",
              "Microsoft YaHei",
          ],
          "macos" => &[
              "Apple SD Gothic Neo",
              "PingFang SC",
              "Hiragino Sans",
          ],
          _ => &[
              "Noto Sans CJK KR",
              "Noto Sans CJK SC",
              "Noto Sans CJK JP",
              "Noto Sans CJK",
          ],
      };

      for name in cjk_candidates {
          if let Some(font) = find_system_font(name) { // platform-specific lookup
              ctx.add_system_font(name, font);
          }
      }

      ctx.set_fallback_order(vec![
          "JetBrains Mono".into(),
          "Inter".into(),
          // Add CJK fallbacks at the end
          "Malgun Gothic".into(),
          "Meiryo".into(),
          "Microsoft YaHei".into(),
          "Apple SD Gothic Neo".into(),
          "PingFang SC".into(),
          "Hiragino Sans".into(),
          "Noto Sans CJK".into(),
      ]);
  }
  ```
- Implement `find_system_font` per OS:
  - On Linux, search common fontconfig paths or call `fc-match` via a small helper; consider using existing font discovery utilities if already present in the codebase.
  - On Windows, use standard font directory and file name mapping for known fonts.
  - On macOS, search `/System/Library/Fonts`, `/Library/Fonts`.
- Ensure that fallback order preserves current appearance for Latin text and only uses CJK fonts when necessary.

Configuration & future-proofing:
- Keep the behavior behind a small abstraction so future decision to bundle a subset CJK font (1–2 MB) or full Noto font can be added by simply registering an additional in-binary font and putting it in the fallback chain.
- Add comments documenting why system fallback is preferred and size implications of bundling.

Backward compatibility:
- Do not change default fonts for non-CJK users.
- Avoid config changes for this feature in this release to keep it simple.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual & automated checks:
- Create a test markdown file containing Korean (한글), Chinese (中文), Japanese (日本語) text as specified in the PRD.
- On Linux (Ubuntu 22.04), Windows, and macOS:
  - Open the file in Ferrite and verify that all CJK characters render as legible glyphs, not boxes.
  - Confirm Latin text still uses the existing primary fonts and appearance is unchanged.
- Negative tests:
  - Temporarily uninstall CJK fonts on a Linux test VM (if feasible) to confirm that behavior fails gracefully (e.g., still boxes, but no crash) and log a helpful message.
- Regression tests:
  - Verify that editor startup time and memory usage are not significantly worse.
  - Ensure there is no change in rendering of non-CJK documents compared to v0.2.1 screenshots.
- If a unit-testable font registry is available, add a small test asserting that fallback order includes at least one CJK font for each platform.

tasks.title: Fix undo/redo behavior preserving scroll and focus
tasks.description: Investigate and fix Ctrl+Z undo/redo behavior so it no longer resets scroll position, loses keyboard focus, or requires double-press to take effect.
tasks.details:
Scope:
- Address Issue #5 by making undo/redo operations minimally mutate state, preserving scroll position, caret position, and focus whenever possible.

Code analysis & design:
- Inspect `src/editor/widget.rs` to understand current event handling for Ctrl+Z / Ctrl+Shift+Z.
- Inspect `src/state.rs` for undo stack, document snapshot, and state mutation logic.
- Identify whether undo currently replaces the entire document state, causing scroll reset and focus loss.

Implementation approach:
- Refactor undo stack from full-document snapshots (if that is the case) to a more localized change model, or ensure that view-related state is preserved across undos.
- Introduce a `ViewState` struct (if not present) to separate **model** vs **view** concerns:
  ```rust
  #[derive(Clone, Debug)]
  pub struct ViewState {
      pub scroll_offset: f32,
      pub cursor_position: CursorPosition,
      pub has_keyboard_focus: bool,
  }

  #[derive(Clone, Debug)]
  pub struct EditorState {
      pub document: Document,
      pub view: ViewState,
      // existing fields...
  }
  ```
- When performing an undo operation:
  - Capture current `ViewState` before applying the undo.
  - Apply the undo to the document/model portion only (text, annotations, syntax state, etc.).
  - Reapply or merge the previous `ViewState` to the new state unless the undo specifically changes cursor/selection location.
- Ensure keyboard focus:
  - Confirm that the editor widget registers itself as the active focus target on key events and that undo does not re-create or re-mount the widget in a way that drops focus.
  - Avoid rebuild logic that destroys the focused widget on state update.

Double-press issue:
- Investigate if the first Ctrl+Z only marks something as dirty or is consumed by another handler (e.g., global shortcut handler vs widget-level handler).
- Ensure a single, centralized handler for undo with clear priority.
- Pseudo-code for event handling:
  ```rust
  fn handle_key_event(&mut self, event: KeyEvent) -> EventResult {
      match event {
          KeyEvent { key: 'Z', modifiers: Modifiers::CTRL } => {
              self.undo();
              EventResult::Handled
          }
          KeyEvent { key: 'Z', modifiers: Modifiers::CTRL_SHIFT } => {
              self.redo();
              EventResult::Handled
          }
          _ => EventResult::Ignored,
      }
  }
  ```

Scroll preservation:
- Ensure that any layout recomputation after undo uses the stored `scroll_offset` as the initial scroll position.
- If text height changes substantially, clamp `scroll_offset` to the new maximum scroll.

Tests & telemetry hooks:
- Consider adding debug logging around undo/redo operations (guarded by log level) to help triage any remaining issues.

Backward compatibility:
- Ensure existing undo/redo functionality still supports multi-step history.
- Do not change shortcut keys.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual scenario tests:
- Open a long document.
- Type some text at the top, then scroll halfway down.
- Delete a line near the top (or at current cursor), then scroll again.
- Press Ctrl+Z once:
  - Text change is undone.
  - Scroll position stays at the same place (or as close as possible given content changes).
  - Editor retains keyboard focus (typing continues to insert text immediately).
- Press Ctrl+Z multiple times to walk back through history; confirm that each press triggers an undo (no double-press requirement).
- Press Ctrl+Shift+Z (or equivalent redo shortcut) to redo operations, again preserving scroll and focus.

Regression checks:
- Ensure undo/redo still works across multiple tabs/documents.
- Confirm no panics in log output related to state cloning or view state.
- If unit tests exist for state management, add tests ensuring view state is preserved across `apply_undo()` calls.

tasks.title: Fix UTF-8-safe string handling in tree viewer
tasks.description: Prevent crashes in the tree viewer when displaying JSON/YAML containing multi-byte UTF-8 characters by replacing byte-based slicing with char/grapheme-aware indexing.
tasks.details:
Scope:
- Address the UTF-8 tree viewer crash caused by invalid byte-based string slicing when rendering JSON/YAML with multi-byte characters (emoji, CJK, Norwegian characters, etc.).

Implementation details:
- Work in `src/markdown/tree_viewer.rs`.
- Locate all usages of `&s[start..end]` where `start` and `end` are derived from byte offsets that might not align with UTF-8 codepoint boundaries.

Safe slicing approach:
- Prefer iteration over `char_indices()` or grapheme clusters depending on how accurate the visual alignment must be.
- For most structural tree views, char-based indexing is sufficient:
  ```rust
  fn safe_substr(s: &str, start_char: usize, end_char: usize) -> &str {
      let mut start_byte = 0;
      let mut end_byte = s.len();
      for (i, (byte_idx, _)) in s.char_indices().enumerate() {
          if i == start_char { start_byte = byte_idx; }
          if i == end_char { end_byte = byte_idx; break; }
      }
      &s[start_byte..end_byte]
  }
  ```
- If the viewer truncates lines by column width, base truncation on `char` count rather than bytes.

Refactoring strategy:
- Introduce a small utility module or functions in `tree_viewer.rs` to handle:
  - Safe substring by character index.
  - Calculation of visible width in characters.
- Replace direct byte slicing with calls into these utilities.

Performance considerations:
- For large files, avoid repeatedly scanning from the beginning for every slice.
- Consider precomputing a vector of `(byte_index, char)` pairs for each line if needed.

Error handling:
- If legacy code passes byte offsets, convert them to char-based indices only once at boundary points.

Backward compatibility:
- Match previous visual behavior (indentation, truncation) as closely as possible while avoiding panics.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests:
- Create JSON/YAML fixtures containing:
  - Norwegian characters: ø, æ, å.
  - Emoji: 🎉, 🚀, 🙂.
  - Chinese/Japanese characters.
- Open each file in the tree viewer:
  - Verify that the viewer no longer crashes or panics.
  - Check that keys/values are rendered correctly and properly aligned.

Automated tests (if test harness exists):
- Add a regression test that:
  - Loads a JSON string with multi-byte characters.
  - Renders or walks through the corresponding tree structure.
  - Asserts that no panic occurs (e.g., by running under test harness with `should_panic` disabled) and that returned strings match expected substrings.

Regression checks:
- Open large JSON/YAML files and confirm performance is acceptable.
- Ensure pure ASCII files render unchanged compared to previous versions.

tasks.title: Integrate clap-based CLI parsing for file and directory arguments
tasks.description: Add clap with derive macros to parse CLI arguments and open files or a directory as tabs/workspace on startup.
tasks.details:
Scope:
- Implement Issue #9: support opening files and directories from the command line using `clap`.

Dependency setup:
- Update `Cargo.toml`:
  ```toml
  [dependencies]
  clap = { version = "^4", features = ["derive"] }
  ```
- Ensure binary size impact is acceptable; use `derive` rather than builder API for simplicity.

Argument model:
- Define a struct for CLI options in `src/main.rs`:
  ```rust
  use clap::{Parser};

  #[derive(Parser, Debug)]
  #[command(name = "Ferrite")]
  pub struct Cli {
      /// Files or directories to open at startup
      pub paths: Vec<std::path::PathBuf>,

      /// Optional log level override (to be wired in Task 28)
      #[arg(long = "log-level", value_enum)]
      pub log_level: Option<LogLevelArg>,
  }

  #[derive(clap::ValueEnum, Clone, Debug)]
  pub enum LogLevelArg {
      Debug,
      Info,
      Warn,
      Error,
      Off,
  }
  ```
- For v0.2.2, you may define `log_level` arg but only fully wire it in Task 28, or leave it out now if strict minimalism is preferred; ensure consistency with Task 28.

Startup integration:
- In `main()`, parse CLI first:
  ```rust
  fn main() {
      let cli = Cli::parse();
      // initialize logging after config is loaded (Task 28)
      // then bootstrap app with initial paths
  }
  ```
- Pass `cli.paths` into the application initialization (likely via `App::new(initial_paths)` or similar in `src/app.rs`).

Behavior:
- `ferrite myfile.md`:
  - Resolve path to absolute path.
  - Open as a single tab.
- `ferrite file1.md file2.md`:
  - Open each file as a separate tab.
- `ferrite .`:
  - Treat a directory argument as a workspace root.
  - Open workspace view (or at minimum remember root dir for file tree/browser); follow existing workspace semantics if already implemented.
- If both files and directories are provided, decide on either:
  - Use the first directory as workspace root and open all files as tabs; or
  - For simplicity in this release, support multiple files or a single directory; document behavior in `--help` text.

Error handling:
- For non-existent paths:
  - Log a warning and skip.
- For directories when workspace mode is not fully implemented:
  - Either open nothing and print a friendly message, or open file tree only.

Backward compatibility:
- Keep behavior unchanged when no arguments are supplied; app starts with default empty state.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests:
- From terminal in various directories:
  - Run `ferrite` → app starts normally, no errors.
  - Run `ferrite README.md` → README.md opens in a tab.
  - Run `ferrite file1.md file2.md` → both files open as separate tabs.
  - Run `ferrite .` → current directory is used as workspace root (or documented behavior).
- Provide a mix of valid and invalid paths, e.g., `ferrite existing.md missing.md` and confirm missing file is logged but does not crash the app.

Regression checks:
- Confirm that GUI launchers (desktop shortcuts) still work as before.
- Ensure that parsing unknown flags or malformed arguments yields a clean error message from `clap` rather than a panic.
- Verify that later tasks (version/help, log-level) still parse correctly with this struct.

tasks.title: Add standard --version and --help CLI flags via clap
tasks.description: Expose conventional --version/-V and --help/-h flags so Ferrite prints version and usage information in the terminal.
tasks.details:
Scope:
- Implement Issue #10 providing standard CLI flags using `clap`.

Implementation details:
- Reuse the `Cli` struct from Task 26 in `src/main.rs`.
- Annotate with `#[command(version, about, long_about = None)]` so `clap` automatically generates `--help` and `--version` logic.
  ```rust
  #[derive(Parser, Debug)]
  #[command(
      name = "ferrite",
      version = env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"),
      about = "Ferrite Markdown Editor",
  )]
  pub struct Cli { /* fields */ }
  ```
- Rely on `clap`’s default behavior:
  - `ferrite --help` or `-h` prints usage and exits with code 0.
  - `ferrite --version` or `-V` prints `Ferrite <version>` based on `CARGO_PKG_VERSION`.

Behavior:
- Ensure that when `--help` or `--version` is provided, the app does **not** start the GUI; `clap` should exit early before `main()` continues.
- Make sure help text documents positional `paths` behavior and any other supported flags (e.g., `--log-level` if implemented).

Backward compatibility:
- No breaking changes expected, since these flags were previously unsupported; any scripts that passed these flags and saw errors will now see proper output.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests:
- Run `ferrite --version` and `ferrite -V`:
  - Confirm output matches `Ferrite 0.2.2` (or whatever version is in `Cargo.toml`).
  - Confirm process exits with status code 0 and no GUI window appears.
- Run `ferrite --help` and `ferrite -h`:
  - Confirm usage text shows positional `paths` argument and any other flags.
  - Confirm process exits with status code 0 and no GUI window appears.

Regression checks:
- Run `ferrite` with no arguments to confirm standard startup.
- Run `ferrite unknownflag` and verify that `clap` displays an error and usage text rather than panicking.
- Ensure about dialog inside the app (Help → About) still shows correct version, and both sources (CLI and UI) are consistent.

tasks.title: Add log_level to config and initialize logging from settings
tasks.description: Introduce a log_level field in config.json and wire it to control runtime log filtering, defaulting to WARN.
tasks.details:
Scope:
- Implement Issue #11: configurable logging verbosity via `config.json`.

Config schema changes:
- In `src/config/settings.rs`, extend the config struct with a new field:
  ```rust
  #[derive(serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize, Debug, Clone)]
  pub struct Settings {
      // existing fields...
      #[serde(default = "default_log_level")]
      pub log_level: LogLevel,
  }

  fn default_log_level() -> LogLevel { LogLevel::Warn }

  #[derive(serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize, Debug, Clone)]
  #[serde(rename_all = "UPPERCASE")]
  pub enum LogLevel {
      Debug,
      Info,
      Warn,
      Error,
      Off,
  }
  ```
- Use `#[serde(default = "default_log_level")]` to ensure **backward compatibility**: existing `config.json` without `log_level` continue to work and default to `WARN`.

Logging initialization:
- In `src/main.rs`, after loading config but before starting the UI, initialize the logger according to `settings.log_level`.
- If using `env_logger`-like crate:
  ```rust
  fn init_logging(settings: &Settings, cli: &Cli) {
      use log::LevelFilter;

      let base_level = match settings.log_level {
          LogLevel::Debug => LevelFilter::Debug,
          LogLevel::Info  => LevelFilter::Info,
          LogLevel::Warn  => LevelFilter::Warn,
          LogLevel::Error => LevelFilter::Error,
          LogLevel::Off   => LevelFilter::Off,
      };

      // Optional CLI override if `--log-level` is supported
      let level = cli.log_level
          .map(|lvl| match lvl {
              LogLevelArg::Debug => LevelFilter::Debug,
              LogLevelArg::Info  => LevelFilter::Info,
              LogLevelArg::Warn  => LevelFilter::Warn,
              LogLevelArg::Error => LevelFilter::Error,
              LogLevelArg::Off   => LevelFilter::Off,
          })
          .unwrap_or(base_level);

      env_logger::Builder::from_default_env()
          .filter_level(level)
          .init();
  }
  ```
- Ensure log output defaults to WARN when no config entry is present.

CLI interaction (optional, aligns with PRD open question):
- If `--log-level` CLI flag is added (see Task 26 structure), treat CLI as an override of config.
- Document precedence: CLI > config file > built-in default.

Config persistence:
- If there is a settings UI, defer exposing `log_level` there for now (PRD only requires config.json support), but ensure serialization/deserialization works correctly.

Backward compatibility & migration:
- When saving config, always include `log_level`.
- When reading old config, rely on serde defaults to populate missing field.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests:
- Delete existing `config.json` and launch Ferrite:
  - Inspect generated config to confirm `"log_level": "WARN"` or equivalent.
  - Observe that terminal output shows only warnings and errors (no debug/info spam).
- Edit `config.json` to each level in turn: DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, OFF:
  - Restart Ferrite and verify log verbosity matches the selected level.
- If CLI override is implemented:
  - Set config to `ERROR` but run `ferrite --log-level DEBUG`; confirm debug logs appear.

Regression checks:
- Ensure that invalid `log_level` values in config produce a sensible error or fall back to default without crashing.
- Confirm that logging initialization happens once and does not panic on re-init (no double `env_logger::init()` calls).

tasks.title: Add default_view_mode setting and apply on new tabs
tasks.description: Introduce a default_view_mode configuration option and apply it when creating new tabs, while allowing per-tab overrides.
tasks.details:
Scope:
- Implement Issue #3 by allowing users to choose a global default view mode for new tabs: Raw, Rendered, or Split.

Config changes:
- In `src/config/settings.rs`, extend settings struct:
  ```rust
  #[derive(serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize, Debug, Clone)]
  pub struct Settings {
      // existing fields...
      #[serde(default = "default_view_mode")] 
      pub default_view_mode: ViewMode,
  }

  fn default_view_mode() -> ViewMode { ViewMode::Raw }

  #[derive(serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize, Debug, Clone, Copy)]
  #[serde(rename_all = "PascalCase")] // or explicit strings: RAW/RENDERED/SPLIT
  pub enum ViewMode {
      Raw,
      Rendered,
      Split,
  }
  ```
- Use serde defaults to keep existing `config.json` files valid and default to current behavior (Raw).

Application integration:
- In `src/app.rs`, find the code path responsible for creating a new tab/document.
- Modify new-tab initialization to read `settings.default_view_mode` and set the tab’s initial view accordingly:
  ```rust
  fn create_tab(&mut self, path: Option<PathBuf>) {
      let view_mode = self.settings.default_view_mode;
      let tab = EditorTab::new(path, view_mode);
      self.tabs.push(tab);
  }
  ```
- Ensure each tab maintains its own view mode; user changes within a tab should not modify global settings.

Settings UI integration:
- In `src/ui/settings.rs`, add a control (e.g., a dropdown) allowing users to pick among Raw / Rendered / Split.
- Bind the UI control to `settings.default_view_mode` and persist changes to `config.json` when users apply settings.

Backward compatibility:
- Keep current behavior for users who never touch settings: new tabs open in Raw mode.
- Ensure `default_view_mode` is always serialized so manual editing of config remains straightforward.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests:
- Delete `config.json` and start Ferrite; open new tabs:
  - Confirm they default to Raw view (current behavior).
- In settings UI:
  - Set default view mode to Split.
  - Save and restart the app (if required by settings persistence).
  - Create several new tabs; verify each opens in Split mode by default.
  - Change one tab to Rendered only and confirm it affects only that tab.
- Set `default_view_mode` to Rendered via direct `config.json` edit and confirm behavior on next launch.

Regression checks:
- Ensure existing open tabs from a previous session (if session restore exists) retain their stored view mode and are not overridden by the global default.
- Confirm that switching view mode within a tab still works and does not modify `default_view_mode` in the config unless explicitly changed via settings UI.

tasks.title: Profile and optimize Mermaid diagram rendering performance
tasks.description: Identify and optimize performance bottlenecks in mermaid.rs when rendering complex diagrams with 50+ nodes.
tasks.details:
Scope:
- Improve rendering performance for complex Mermaid diagrams as described in the PRD, without changing external behavior.

Profiling setup:
- Work in `src/markdown/mermaid.rs`.
- Add optional, feature-gated instrumentation or use existing profiling tools (e.g., `cargo flamegraph`, `perf`) to identify hot paths.
- Focus on diagrams with 50+ nodes, many edges, and nested subgraphs.

Potential optimization areas (to verify via profiling):
- Parsing:
  - Avoid repeated allocations for tokens; reuse buffers where possible.
  - Replace naive string splitting with more efficient iterators.
- Layout:
  - Cache layout computations for nodes/edges where recomputation is unnecessary across minor document edits.
  - Avoid O(N^2) loops if possible; use indexed data structures.
- Rendering:
  - Batch draw calls where the underlying graphics library benefits from batching.
  - Reuse shape/path objects instead of constructing them from scratch on each frame.

Refactoring patterns:
- Introduce internal structs for parsed diagrams:
  ```rust
  struct Diagram {
      nodes: Vec<Node>,
      edges: Vec<Edge>,
      // layout metadata
  }
  ```
- Separate parsing from layout from rendering to reduce cross-coupled work and enable caching at each stage (see Task 31 for further modularity).

Caching strategy (lightweight for this release):
- Cache the parsed diagram per code block (e.g., keyed by a hash of the mermaid source string).
- On text change, recompute only for modified code blocks.

Safety & correctness:
- Ensure optimizations do not change visual output of diagrams.
- Keep changes localized to `mermaid.rs` to avoid ripple effects.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual performance tests:
- Create or use existing Mermaid examples with:
  - 10 nodes (small diagram).
  - 50+ nodes with many edges (stress test).
- Compare rendering responsiveness between v0.2.1 behavior and optimized version (informal side-by-side if possible):
  - Time to first render after opening document.
  - Responsiveness while editing mermaid code (typing, scrolling).

Profiling validation:
- Run profiling before and after changes to confirm reduction in time spent in hot functions.
- Record qualitative improvements (e.g., CPU usage, frame time) for internal documentation.

Regression checks:
- Open simple diagrams and confirm they render identically.
- Ensure no new panics, layout glitches, or incorrect edge routing appear in diagrams.
- Run the general app test suite (if available) to ensure no impact on non-Mermaid markdown rendering.

tasks.title: Refactor mermaid.rs for code cleanliness and modularity
tasks.description: Clean up unused code and split the monolithic mermaid.rs into focused submodules (parser, layout, render) with documentation comments.
tasks.details:
Scope:
- Improve code quality in `src/markdown/mermaid.rs` as per PRD by removing dead code, modularizing, and documenting main components.

Refactoring plan:
- Analyze current `mermaid.rs` to identify logical layers:
  - Parsing of mermaid syntax into an intermediate representation.
  - Layout computation for nodes/edges.
  - Rendering of the layout into the UI.
- Create a `src/markdown/mermaid/` module directory with submodules:
  - `parser.rs`
  - `layout.rs`
  - `render.rs`
  - `mod.rs` that re-exports public API.

Step-by-step:
- Move parsing functions and types into `parser.rs`, keeping public entry points like `parse_diagram(source: &str) -> Diagram`.
- Move layout logic into `layout.rs` with main function `layout_diagram(diagram: &mut Diagram, constraints: &LayoutConfig)`.
- Move rendering code (interacting with the graphics/UI layer) into `render.rs`.
- Update call sites to use `mermaid::render_mermaid_block(...)` or similar top-level API, consolidating external surface area.

Dead-code removal:
- Run `cargo deadlinks` / `cargo check` / `cargo clippy` to identify unused functions and types.
- Remove or privatize dead code.
- For code that is not currently used but might be relevant soon, add `#[allow(dead_code)]` plus a comment explaining why it is kept.

Documentation:
- Add module-level and function-level Rustdoc comments:
  ```rust
  /// Parses Mermaid syntax into an internal diagram representation.
  pub fn parse_diagram(source: &str) -> Result<Diagram, ParseError> { ... }
  ```
- Document high-level flow in `mod.rs` so future contributors can quickly understand architecture.

Coordination with Task 30:
- Ensure that performance improvements from Task 30 either leverage or are preserved by this refactor.
- If necessary, do minimal structural changes first, then optimize.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual & automated regression tests:
- Rebuild the project and run all existing tests (unit/integration) to confirm no breakage from module moves.
- Open several markdown files with Mermaid code examples and verify diagrams render correctly.

Quality checks:
- Run `cargo clippy` and address warnings in the new modules.
- Run `cargo doc` to ensure documentation builds and key public functions are documented.

Regression & coupling checks:
- Confirm no other modules import low-level internals directly (enforce a clean public API via `mod.rs`).
- Verify that future changes (e.g., additional diagram types) can be implemented by extending parser/layout/render with minimal cross-module changes.

tasks.title: Review and resolve external PRs for v0.2.2 release
tasks.description: Review, coordinate with contributors, and resolve the two pending external pull requests (CJK font support and macOS binary naming) so they are either merged or closed for v0.2.2.
tasks.details:
Scope:
- Own the lifecycle of the two external PRs blocking or related to the v0.2.2 release: PR #8 (CJK font support, linked to Issue #7) and PR #2 (macOS binary naming and architecture support).
- Ensure each PR is either merged (with any required follow-up tasks created) or closed with clear rationale.
- Keep Issue references (Issue #7 and any macOS/packaging issues) up to date with the final decisions.

PR #8 – CJK Font Support (Issue #7):
1. Review current PR implementation:
   - Confirm which CJK fonts are bundled, where they are stored, and how they are wired into the font initialization/rendering pipeline (e.g., `src/fonts.rs` or equivalent).
   - Measure and document binary size impact (confirm the reported ~15.7 MB increase, using `du`, `cargo bloat`, or build artifacts) and note platform-specific variations.
   - Verify that CJK text (Korean, Chinese, Japanese) renders correctly on all supported platforms with the PR as-is.
2. Coordinate with contributor:
   - Summarize current concerns in a PR comment: binary size increase, overlap with planned system font fallback (Task 23), and any performance implications.
   - Ask the contributor to clarify whether they can:
     - Provide a subsetted font to reduce binary size, or
     - Adjust the implementation to prefer system fonts and only fall back to bundled fonts when necessary.
   - Set expectations about response time and how long the PR will be kept open while awaiting feedback.
3. Decide path based on contributor response:
   - If contributor responds and proposes an acceptable solution:
     - Review updated changes, focusing on:
       - Compatibility with the planned system fallback design in Task 23 (avoid APIs or data structures that would hinder system-first behavior).
       - Maintained or improved CJK rendering quality.
       - Controlled binary size growth.
     - Request any minor follow-up changes (naming, code structure, comments) and re-review until ready to merge.
     - Once approved, merge the PR and create/adjust follow-up work:
       - If necessary, refine or narrow Task 23 to focus on system fallback layering on top of the merged behavior.
   - If contributor does not respond within the agreed window or cannot provide an acceptable alternative:
     - Document in the PR why it cannot be merged in its current form (binary size, conflicting direction with system fallback).
     - Decide whether to:
       - Close the PR in favor of implementing the desired system font fallback in Task 23, or
       - Temporarily defer and explicitly label it as “on hold” with a clear date for re-evaluation.
4. Documentation and linkage:
   - Update Issue #7 with a comment explaining the final decision, referencing the PR discussion.
   - If the PR is merged, document the behavior and size tradeoffs in the changelog for v0.2.2 and/or in developer docs.
   - If closed, ensure Task 23 explicitly covers the required behavior that remains unimplemented.

PR #2 – macOS Binary Naming and Architecture Support:
1. Review current PR changes:
   - Inspect how the macOS binary or artifact is named (e.g., `macos-arm64`, `macos-x64`, `universal`, etc.).
   - Confirm via CI configuration or local builds which architectures are currently produced (ARM only vs ARM + Intel).
   - Identify any mismatches between naming and actual architecture support (e.g., renamed to `macos-arm64` but still only building for one arch, or potential confusion for users).
2. Coordinate with contributor:
   - Comment on the PR summarizing current behavior and the expected outcomes:
     - Option A: Build separate ARM and Intel artifacts with clear, architecture-specific names.
     - Option B: Keep ARM-only builds but ensure naming clearly indicates ARM-only support.
   - Ask the contributor which approach they prefer and if they are willing to adjust CI or build scripts accordingly.
   - Clarify any release or distribution constraints (e.g., CI time, complexity of universal binaries) that might impact the decision.
3. Decide and finalize implementation:
   - If the contributor updates the PR:
     - Verify that build pipelines succeed for the configured macOS targets.
     - Confirm that artifact names match the actual architectures produced and are consistent with existing release notes or packaging conventions.
     - Request minor cleanups (comments, script robustness, handling of missing toolchains) as needed, then approve and merge.
   - If the contributor does not respond or cannot make required changes:
     - Decide whether maintainers should push minimal corrective commits to the PR branch (if project norms allow) or close the PR and open a new internal task for macOS artifact naming.
     - Document the reasoning in the PR discussion.
4. Documentation and release integration:
   - Update any release or packaging documentation to describe the final macOS artifact naming and supported architectures for v0.2.2.
   - Ensure CI configuration, release scripts, and GitHub Releases (or equivalent) are aligned with the new naming scheme.

General process and coordination:
- Maintain a short internal note or checklist tracking for each PR: status (awaiting response, changes requested, ready to merge), last contact date, and decision deadline.
- Keep communication polite and explicit about what is needed to move forward.
- Coordinate with whoever owns release management for v0.2.2 to ensure these PRs are resolved before tagging the release.
- If either PR is closed without merging, create or update internal tasks to capture any remaining work so that functionality is not lost.

Completion criteria:
- PR #8 is either merged (with outcome documented and any follow-up tasks filed) or closed with a clear explanation and Issue #7 updated accordingly.
- PR #2 is either merged with correct macOS naming/architecture semantics or closed with rationale and a replacement plan.
- v0.2.2 release checklist reflects the final state of both PRs.
- This task is marked done once both PRs are in a resolved state (merged or closed).
tasks.testStrategy:
Since this task is largely about review and coordination, validation focuses on repository state, CI, and behavior of resulting builds.

PR #8 – CJK Font Support:
- After a final decision is implemented (merge or close):
  - If merged:
    - Build release binaries for all supported platforms.
    - Measure binary size and compare it against the previous release to confirm the expected size impact is documented.
    - Open a markdown file containing Korean, Chinese, and Japanese sample text and verify that all characters render correctly without missing-glyph boxes on Linux, Windows, and macOS.
    - Run existing automated tests (unit/integration) involving fonts or rendering and ensure they pass.
  - If closed:
    - Confirm that the PR is closed in the repository with a comment summarizing why, and that Issue #7 and Task 23 reflect the chosen direction (system font fallback).

PR #2 – macOS Binary Naming:
- After a final decision is implemented (merge or close):
  - Trigger or inspect CI builds for macOS and confirm:
    - The produced artifacts match the intended architecture support (ARM-only, Intel-only, or both).
    - Artifact filenames clearly indicate architecture (e.g., `ferrite-macos-arm64`, `ferrite-macos-x86_64`, or equivalent) and match documented expectations.
  - Download the macOS artifacts and:
    - Verify that they run successfully on the corresponding hardware (ARM and/or Intel Macs as applicable).
    - Confirm that `--version` reports the correct v0.2.2 version string.
  - Ensure CI passes and release packaging scripts use the updated names without failure.

Overall task verification:
- Confirm that both PR #8 and PR #2 are in a terminal state (merged or closed) in the repository UI.
- Confirm that associated issues (e.g., Issue #7) and internal tasks are updated with links to the resolved PRs and a brief summary of the outcome.
- Confirm that the v0.2.2 release notes or changelog correctly describe any user-visible changes related to CJK font handling and macOS binary naming/architecture support.

tasks.title: Fix misleading code folding UI
tasks.description: Hide non-functional fold/collapse buttons by default and clean up the Rendered JSON view to prevent user confusion (Issue #12).
tasks.details:
The code folding feature shows fold indicators in the editor gutter, but clicking them doesn't actually collapse the code (text hiding deferred to v0.3.0). This confuses users who expect the collapse to work.

Changes needed:

1. **Hide fold/collapse buttons by default**
   - Remove the fold gutter indicators from the editor by default since they don't function
   - File: `src/editor/folding.rs`

2. **Add setting to enable fold indicators**
   - Add a setting in Editor settings: `show_fold_indicators` (default: false)
   - Allow power users to see fold regions even without collapse functionality
   - Files: `src/config/settings.rs`, `src/ui/settings.rs`

3. **Remove 'Raw View' button from Rendered JSON/YAML view**
   - The button shows uneditable raw text which isn't useful
   - Users should use the view mode selector to switch to Raw mode for editing
   - File: `src/markdown/tree_viewer.rs`

GitHub Issue: #12
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual tests:
- Open JSON file in editor
- Verify NO fold indicators appear in the gutter by default
- Go to Settings > Editor, enable 'Show fold indicators'
- Verify fold indicators now appear (but still don't collapse - expected for v0.2.2)
- Open JSON in Rendered view
- Verify no 'Raw View' button is present
- Use view mode selector to switch between Raw/Rendered - should work normally
